stimulus: Locke believed that the mind at birth is a blank slate, with all knowledge arriving through sensory experience. In his view, no innate principles exist; rather, ideas are built up from impressions of the world. Leibniz disagreed, arguing that some structures—such as basic logical and mathematical truths—must be innate to the mind itself; ______blank certain ideas precede and shape any experience of the world.
stem: Which choice completes the text with the most logical transition?
rationale:
Choice C is the best answer. The clause after the blank restates Leibniz's position in plainer terms: innate structures shape any experience. “In other words” introduces a restatement, which is exactly what the sentence does.

Choice A signals indifference to a prior point; nothing is being dismissed. Choice B concedes; no concession is offered. Choice D signals simultaneity, which doesn't fit.
